IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
          CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.______/2026
@ SLP (CRL.) NO.______/2026
@ DIARY NO.  4326/2026
RIJUL CHAUDHARY                          APPELLANT
                          VERSUS
STATE OF HIMACHAL PRADESH               RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Learned counsel appearing for the petitioner
submits that the advance copy has been served on
respondent on an incorrect e-mail. In view of the
same, question of respondent being aware of the
pendency of this petition would not arise. 
2. At this juncture, Mr. Vaibhav Srivastava,
learned AAG appears on behalf of the respondent –
State and submits that he has received copy of
the petition. 
3. Heard. 
1

--- Page 230 ---
4. Delay condoned. 
5. Leave granted. 
6. Appellant who came to be tried for the
offence punishable under Section 20(b)(ii)(c) and
Section 29 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic
Substances Act, 1985 has been sentenced to 10
years  of  rigorous  imprisonment  and  fine  of
Rs.1,00,000/-. 
7. Appeal filed challenging the same is pending
before the High Court and the application for
suspension of sentence has been rejected. 
8. Having heard the learned Additional Advocate
General appearing for the respondent - State, who
has opposed the suspension of sentence and grant
of  bail,  by  vehemently  contending  that  the
contraband  seized  was  from  the  conscious
possession of the appellant and the commercial
quantity being 1 kg, the seized contraband was
1.505 kg and as such appellant is not entitled
for the relief sought for. He prays for dismissal
of the appeal. 
2

--- Page 231 ---
9. Having heard the learned counsel appearing
for the appellant, we notice that out of 10 years
sentence imposed on the appellant, he has already
completed 4 years 8 months, i.e., around 50% of
the  sentence  awarded.  The  appeal  is  still
pending before the High Court. Hence, any opinion
expressed with regard to the rival contentions
would definitely prejudice the rights of either
of the parties. Hence, without going into these
aspects and keeping in mind that appeal being
disposed of in the near future being remote and
there being no antecedents attributable to the
appellant of having committed similar offences,
we are of the considered view that sentence
imposed on the appellant deserves to be suspended
and accordingly, the sentence imposed on the
appellant  stands  suspended  and  appellant  is
ordered to be released on bail on such terms and
conditions as the trial court may deem fit to
impose. Accordingly, the appeal stands allowed.
3

--- Page 232 ---
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
dispose of. 
....................J.  
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
.....................J.  
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)  
NEW DELHI;
MAY 26, 2026

1. Leave granted.

the High Court of Andhra Pradesh at Amaravati.

order:-
“1.  The  petitioners  challenge  the
judgment  and  order  dated  18.03.2026
passed  by  the  High  Court  of  Andhra
Pradesh at Amaravati in Criminal Petition
1

--- Page 236 ---
No.1787  of  2026,  titled  “Pami  Reddy
Viswanath Reddy and Ors. vs. The State of
Andhra Pradesh”. 
2. We have heard learned counsel for
the parties.
3.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
26.05.2026.
4. Mr.  Guntur  Pramod  Kumar,  learned
Advocate-on-Record,  accepts  notice  on
behalf of the respondent-State of Andhra
Pradesh.  Hence,  the  formal  service  of
notice on the respondent-State of Andhra
Pradesh is waived.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioners
to serve a copy of the present petition
to  the  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent-State of Andhra Pradesh within
a period of one week.
6. Let  counter  affidavit  be  filed
within  two  weeks.  Rejoinder  affidavit
thereto, if any, be filed within one week
thereafter.
7. We make it clear that no further
opportunity  will  be  granted  for  this
purpose.
8. In the event of arrest in connection
with FIR No.10 of 2026, dated 22.01.2026,
registered  at  Police  Station
Peddavadugur, District Ananthapuram, the
petitioners shall be released on bail on
the appropriate terms and conditions as
may  be  fixed  by  the  Investigating
Officer/Arresting  Officer.  However,  in
the  attending  facts,  one  of  the
conditions shall necessarily be that if
2

--- Page 237 ---
the petitioners possess a passport, the
same  shall  be  deposited  before  the
Competent  Authority/Court,  till  the
conclusion  of  the  Trial  or  directed
otherwise.
9. The  petitioners  shall  make
themselves  available  before  the
Investigating  Officer  on  11.05.2026  at
10:00 a.m. and on all such dates as they
may be required.
10.  Needless  to  add,  till  the
investigation  is  not  complete  in  all
respects,  the  petitioners  would  fully
cooperate,  and  if  the  challan/charge
sheet is filed, they would maintain good
conduct and not attempt to influence any
of the witnesses in any manner till the
completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellants have cooperated during the
investigation; they have not threatened or intimidated
any of the witnesses; or have tried to influence the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeals by confirming the order
dated 29.04.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellants to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
3

--- Page 238 ---
adjournment. Also, the appellants shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellants’ conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 18.03.2026 is
set aside.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.12305/2025
ANAND & ANR.                …APPELLANT(S) 
VERSUS
STATE OF KARNATAKA         …RESPONDENT(S)
WITH
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO. 14823/2025
O R D E R
1. Heard. 
2. Leave granted. 
3. The facts shorn of unwarranted details are as under:
The appellants who were arraigned as accused in the FIR
registered for the offences punishable under Sections 143, 147,
148, 323, 324, 504 and 506 read with 149 of the Indian Penal
1

--- Page 242 ---
Code, 1860 (“IPC”) and Section 3(1)(10)(14) of the Scheduled
Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act are
before this Court challenging the judgment of conviction and
sentence imposed on them which came to be affirmed by the
High Court. 
4. Perusal  of  the  records  would  disclose  that  after  full-
fledged trial, appellants came to be convicted by the trial court
by  judgment  dated  24.08.2012.  During  the  pendency  of  the
proceedings before the High Court, the sentence was suspended
and  regular  bail  came  to  be  granted  vide  order  dated
09.10.2012. When the matter was listed for final hearing before
the High Court on 10.06.2025, the learned counsel representing
the  appellants  herein  (accused)  for  reasons  best  known  was
absent. It is very heartening to note that though the counsel was
called twice to appear before the Court, he remained absent. In
fact, the learned counsel appearing for the appellant submits
that  appellants  are  ready  and  willing  to  appear  through  the
counsel  on  any  day  that  may  be  fixed  by  this  Court.  His
submission and undertaking is placed on record. 
2

--- Page 243 ---
5. Thus, the course which was left open to the appellate
court in situation aforesaid was to either seek the services of a
panel counsel from the High Court Legal Services Committee
or appoint an Amicus Curiae for conducting the case, as has
been  held  by  catena  of  judgments  of  this  Court.  However,
surprisingly the High Court proceeded to adjudicate the appeal
on  merits  even  in  the  absence  of  the  learned  counsel  and
without appointing an Amicus Curiae or taking the services of
panel  counsel  from  High  Court  Legal  Services  Committee.
Hence,  on  this  short  ground  itself,  the  impugned  judgment
cannot be sustained. Hence, we allow these appeals by setting
aside the impugned judgment dated 10.06.2025 and remand the
matter to the High Court without expressing any opinion on the
merits of the case or the judgment of the trial court which had
recorded a finding as to the guilt of the accused and leaving
open  all  contention  for  both  parties  to  be  urged  before  the
appellate court as the matter is being remanded back to the High
Court. In the event of the appellants not appearing before the
High  Court  on  the  next  date  of  hearing  which  is  fixed  as
3

--- Page 244 ---
08.06.2026, the High Court would be at liberty to appoint an
Amicus Curiae or take the services of a panel counsel from the
High  Court  Legal  Services  Committee  and  proceed  to
adjudicate the appeal on its merits. Accordingly, the appeals
stand disposed of. 
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of. 
     .............................................J.
(ARA VIND KUMAR)
.............................................J.
(PRASANNA B. V ARALE)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 25, 2026.
4

--- Page 245 ---

[Arising out of SLP (Crl.) No.6013/2026]
 ANKITA PANDEY       APPELLANT
                VERSUS
STATE OF U.P .             RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The High Court of Judicature at Allahabad,
by the impugned judgment and order dated 10 th
March, 2026, has rejected the appellant’s second
application for grant of bail. 
3. Appellant,  figuring  as  an  accused  in  FIR
No.0013  of  2024  dated  23 rd November,  2024
registered  at  Police  Station  Anti-Corruption
Gorakhpur,  District  Gorakhpur,  Uttar  Pradesh
under  Sections  7,  13(1)(b)  and  13(2)  of  the
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6013/2026                              1

--- Page 248 ---
Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988 , was arrested
on 23rd November, 2024.
4. We  have  heard  learned  counsel  for  the
parties.
5.  Chargesheet has been filed on 20th January,
2025.  Prosecution  proposes  to  examine  18
witnesses to drive home the charges against the
appellant,  out  of  whom  evidence  of  only  03
witnesses  appear  to  have  been  recorded  till
date.
6.  T rial  is  progressing  at  a  slow  pace  and
having regard to the present status of the trial, it
appears that it will take some time to conclude.
That apart, the appellant is a woman entitled to
the  benefit  of  Section  480  of  the  Bharatiya
Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.
7. T aking an overall view of the matter, we are
of the considered opinion that further detention
of the appellant pending trial is not necessary;
and, since the appeal deserves acceptance, the
appellant may be admitted to an order for grant
of bail.
8. Accordingly,  we  set  aside  the  impugned
judgment and order.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6013/2026                              2

--- Page 249 ---
9. Appellant shall be released on bail, subject
to furnishing of bail bonds to the satisfaction of
the trial court and subject to such other terms
and conditions as may be imposed by it.
10. Needless to observe, the appellant shall not,
directly  or  indirectly,  by  making  inducement,
threat  or  promise,  dissuade  any  person
acquainted  with  the  facts  of  the  case  from
disclosing such facts to the court.
11. In  the  event  there  is  any  breach  of  the
terms and conditions for grant of bail, the trial
court shall be at liberty to cancel the bail of the
appellant.
12. It  is  also  ordered  that the  appellant shall
diligently attend proceedings of the trial, unless
exempted.  If  she  abstains  from  attending  the
proceedings without justifiable cause, that could
also  be  seen  as  breach  of  the  conditions  for
grant of bail and the trial court will be free to
pass appropriate orders.
13. We  clarify  that  the  observations  made  in
this order and grant of bail will not be treated as
findings on the merits of the case.
Crl. Appeal @ SLP (Crl.) No.6013/2026                              3

--- Page 250 ---
14. The appeal is, accordingly, allowed on the
aforesaid terms.
15. Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall  stand
disposed of.

 (arising out of SLP (Crl.) No. 8434/2026)
MITHLESH TIWARI APPELLANT(S)

THE STATE OF CHHATTISGARH RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1.Leave granted.
2. The  appellant,  Mithlesh  Tiwari,  is
aggrieved  by  the  denial  of  regular
bail by the High Court of Chhattisgarh
at  Bilaspur,  vide order  dated
11.09.2025  passed  in  MCRC  No.
6481/2025.  The  appellant,  Mithilesh
Tiwari,  seeks  bail  in  relation  to
First  Information  Report 1/Crime  No.
91/2025, dated 24.02.2025, registered
1  For short “FIR”
   1

--- Page 254 ---
with Police Station – Sakri, District
–  Bilaspur,  Chhattisgarh,  for  the
offence punishable under Section 21(c)
of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic
Substances Act, 19852. 
3. The  appellant  has  been  in  custody
since 24.02.2025. It is unclear  as to
whether the trial has commenced as on
date. 
4. Learned counsel for the respondent-
State of Chhattisgarh had taken time
on the last occasion to file a counter
affidavit but did not choose to do so
till date. 
5. The  offence  alleged  against  the
appellant arises under the provisions
of the NDPS Act in relation to seizure
of ‘Cough Syrup’. 
6. It  is  an  admitted  fact  that  the
appellant has no past antecedents. As
15 listed witnesses have been cited
for examination by the prosecution,
2  For short “NDPS”
   2

--- Page 255 ---
there is no possibility of the trial
concluding anytime in the near future.
7. Given the aforestated facts, we are of
the opinion that no grounds are made
out for continued incarceration of the
appellant,  Mithlesh  Tiwari,  pending
completion of the trial. 
8. Accordingly,  we  accept  the  present
appeal and direct that the appellant,
Mithlesh Tiwari, shall be released on
bail  in  connection  with  the
aforestated FIR on such appropriate
terms and conditions as may be fixed
by the Special Court. 
9.The impugned order is set aside and
the  appeal  is  allowed  in  the
aforestated terms. 
10.We clarify that we have not made any
observations/comments on the merits of
the case and any observation made in
this  order  is  meant  only  for  the
limited purpose of grant of bail.
   3

--- Page 256 ---
11.Pending application(s), if any, shall

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION NO.1249 OF 2026
IN
REVIEW PETITION (CIVIL) NO. 621 OF 2021
IN
CIVIL APPEAL NO. 1698 OF 2020
DHEERAJ MOR PETITIONER(S)/
APPLICANT(S)
VERSUS
HON’BLE HIGH COURT OF DELHI & 
ORS. RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. We have heard Mr. V. Giri, learned senior counsel for the
applicant-petitioner. We have also considered the claim of the
other similarly placed persons.
2. The five-Judge Constitution Bench in Rejanish K.V. v. K. Deepa
& Ors., 2025 SCC OnLine SC 2196, has categorically held as follows:
“170. In any case, we clarify that what we have held in
this judgment will be applicable only from the date of
this judgment and in no case, any selection process
completed,  or  any  appointment  made  prior  to  this
judgment would be affected, except in cases wherein any
interim order(s) were passed by the High Courts or this
Court. In such cases, the issue would now be governed by
the  orders  to  be  passed  by  the  Bench  hearing  the
matters.
xxx xxx xxx
172. In the result, we answer the questions as under: 
(i) Judicial Officers who have already completed seven
years  in  Bar  before  they  were  recruited  in  the
subordinate judicial service would be entitled for being
appointed as a District Judge/Additional District Judge
1

--- Page 272 ---
in the selection process for the post of District Judges
in the direct recruitment process;
(ii)  The  eligibility  for  appointment  as  a  District
Judge/Additional District Judge is to be seen at the
time of application;
(iii) Though there is no eligibility prescribed under
Article 233(2) for a person already in judicial service
of the Union or of the State for being appointed as
District Judge, in order to provide a level playing
field, we direct that a candidate applying as an in-
service  candidate  should  have  seven  years’  combined
experience as a Judicial Officer and an advocate;
(iv) A person who has been or who is in judicial service
and has a combined experience of seven years or more as
an advocate or a Judicial Officer would be eligible for
being  considered  and  appointed  as  a  District
Judge/Additional District Judge under Article 233 of the
Constitution;
(v) In order to ensure level playing field, we further
direct that the minimum age for being considered and
appointed as a District Judge/Additional District Judge
for both advocates and Judicial Officers would be 35
years of age as on the date of application.
(vi) It is held that the view taken in the judgments of
this Court right from Satya Narain Singh (supra) till
Dheeraj Mor (supra), which take a view contrary to what
has been held hereinabove do not lay down the correct
proposition of law.
xxx xxx xxx
174. Consequently, all such rules framed by the State
Governments in consultation with the High Courts which
are not in accordance with the aforesaid answers shall
stand quashed and set aside. It is directed that all the
State Governments in consultation with the High Courts
shall frame/amend the rules in accordance with what has
been held by us hereinabove, within a period of three
months from today.”
2

--- Page 273 ---
3. On a bare reading of paragraph 170, there is no doubt that the
judgment has to be effected prospectively, w.e.f. 09.10.2025. The
Bench, however, carved out an exception in favour of those where
“any selection process [was] completed, or any appointment [was]
made prior to this judgment...”. In other words, if a direct
recruit or even a serving judicial officer, who had been permitted
to participate in the selection process, and had been selected or
appointed,  all  such  selections  and  appointments  have  been
protected. Such selected officers are entitled to appointment and
if  such  appointed  officers  were  relieved  on  the  ground  of
eligibility, they are also entitled to seek reinstatement. However,
in terms of paragraph 170, reproduced above, it is clear that on
the basis of selection which would be contingent on the outcome of
an incomplete selection process and a anticipated consequential
appointment, no relief of deemed seniority can be granted. This
principle  has  also  been  given  shape  to  in  our  order  dated
11.03.2026.
4. All the High Courts are, accordingly, directed to review the
individual cases of the judicial officers and take an appropriate
decision in terms of the law laid down by the Constitution Bench.
  
5. IA No. 93245/2026 and the miscellaneous application, thus,
stand disposed of.
3

--- Page 274 ---
6. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand disposed of.
..........................CJI.
       (SURYA KANT)
..........................J.
       (JOYMALYA BAGCHI)
..........................J.
       (VIPUL M. PANCHOLI)
 
NEW DELHI;
MAY 22, 2026.
4

--- Page 275 ---

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.      OF 2026
(Arising out of SLP(C) No.5835/2026)
THE STATE OF HIMACHAL PRADESH & ORS. APPELLANT(S)
                              VERSUS
NARESH SHARMA RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. On being pointed out, paragraph 4 of the order
dated 12.02.2026 is corrected. The words “appellate
powers” shall be read as “adjudicatory powers”. Ordered
accordingly.
2. Leave granted.
3. The only issue that had arisen for consideration
before the High Court was whether the State Government
could be permitted to shift the office of Himachal
Pradesh Real Estate Regulatory Authority (in short,
“HPRERA”) from Shimla to Dharamshala.
4. It seems that the shifting of the office has
been opposed primarily on the ground that 90% of the
1

--- Page 278 ---
complaints  pending  or  being  filed  before  HPRERA
primarily pertain to the area in and around Shimla. It
is, therefore, submitted that extreme hardship will be
caused  to  the  complainants  and  the  Advocates
representing them because of having to travel all the
way  from  Shimla  to  Dharamshala  for  pursuing  such
complaints.
5. It  goes  without  saying  that  shifting  the
headquarters of an office/institution is essentially a
policy matter, and if the elected Government decides to
shift an office from one place to another, we see
hardly  any  scope  for  judicial  review  of  such  a
decision. It is in this backdrop that,  vide interim
order dated 12.02.2026, the impugned interim order of
the High Court, whereby stay had been granted in favour
of the Respondents, was stayed. Consequentially, the
State Government was permitted to shift the HPRERA
office from Shimal to Dharamshala.
6. Ms.  Madhavi  Divan,  learned  Senior  Counsel,
appearing for the Appellant-State of Himachal Pradesh,
informs that most of the work for shifting has been
undertaken and the office will become functional at
Dharamshala very soon.
7. We are conscious of the inconvenience that may
be caused to the project proponents, home-buyers, other
complainants, and especially the advocates from Shimla
2

--- Page 279 ---
representing  them,  who  might  face  difficulty  in
travelling to Dharamshala. In this regard, we direct
the State of Himachal Pradesh to ensure that an online
portal  for  filing  of  complaints  and  facilitating
virtual hearings and other online facilities is set up
in  connection  with  the  office  of  the  HPRERA  at
Dharamshala, thereby permitting the advocates or the
complainants from Shimla region to appear online for
pursuing their complaints/cases. Such an arrangement,
we are quite sure, will redress the grievances of the
appellants or other affected persons in and around
Shimla.
8. Having passed the above directions and for the
reasons mentioned above, we see no reason for the
proceedings before the High Court to continue. CWPIL
No.38/2025, accordingly, stands disposed of. This order
shall be placed on the file of the Writ Petition before
the High Court.
9. In the event of any hardship being experienced
by  any  stakeholder,  liberty  is  granted  to  seek
redressal  of  such  grievances  before  the  competent
authority. The State Government will ensure that if any
genuine issue arises, the same is resolved without any
delay.
10. The appeal is, accordingly, disposed of.

ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT REGISTRAR

6

--- Page 283 ---
2026 INSC 563
NON-REPORTABLE
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL ORIGINAL JURISDICTION
MISCELLANEOUS APPLICATION DIARY NO. 31664 OF 2026
IN
WRIT PETITION (CRIMINAL) No. 67 OF 2025
AMIT KATYAL & ANR. PETITIONER(S)/
APPLICANT(S)
          VERSUS
STATE OF HARYANA & ANR.        RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Heard Mr. Siddharth Luthra, learned senior counsel
appearing for the petitioners/applicants and Mr. S.V.
Raju, learned Additional Solicitor General appearing for
the respondent(s).
2. This Miscellaneous Application has been moved for
the clarification/modification of the judgment and order
dated 18.05.2026 delivered by this Court wherein apart
from clubbing of the FIRs, a direction was issued to
club FIR No. 30 of 2019 registered at the PS Economic
Offences Wing, Delhi with FIR No. 439/2024, PS Sector
65, Gurugram, Haryana.
1

--- Page 284 ---
3. This Court while passing the judgment and order
dated 18.05.2026 has observed in Paragraph 26 that FIR
bearing No. 439/2024 registered at Police Station Sector
65, Gurugram, Haryana arises out of the same set of
allegations and forms part of the same transaction which
is  already  the  subject  matter  of  FIR  No.  30/2019
registered at the Economic Offences Wing (EOW), Delhi
and therefore permitting the parallel investigation is
not proper.
4. This Court further in paragraph 27 allowed the
prayer (A) as made in the writ petition which is to the
effect that FIR No. 439/2024, PS Sector 65 Gurugram,
Haryana be clubbed with FIR No. 30 of 2019, PS Economic
Offences Wing (EOW), Delhi.
5. In view of the contents of paragraphs 26 and 27, the
relief which has ultimately been granted by this Court
appears to be incorrect inasmuch as instead of clubbing
and  transferring  FIR  No.439/24  at  PS  Sector  65,
Gurugram,  Haryana,  the  operative  part  directs  for
clubbing  and  transferring  of  FIR  No.  30/2019,  PS
Economic Offences Wing (EOW), Delhi to that with the FIR
No.439/24, PS Sector 65, Gurugram, Haryana.
6. The relief granted has to be in consonance with the
reasoning contained in the body of the judgment and as
2

--- Page 285 ---
such we are of the opinion that that relief portion of
the judgment and order dated 18.05.2026 requires to be
corrected/modified and instead of directing FIR No.
30/2019, PS Economic Offences Wing (EOW), Delhi be
clubbed and transferred to FIR No. 439/2024, PS Sector
65 Gurugram, Haryana, the FIR No. 439/2024 ,PS Sector 65
Gurugram, Haryana ought to be clubbed and transferred to
FIR No. 30/2019, PS Economic Offences Wing (EOW), Delhi.
7. Shri Raju has resisted the proposed modification
contending that the accused cannot be permitted to
choose the investigating agency.
8.  The clarification/modification application is not
seeking any relief so as to select the investigating
agency. The investigation in the offences is being
carried  at  two  places  and  the  court  has  directed
clubbing of the investigation. However, after clubbing
the investigation in respect of the FIR earlier in point
of time, had been directed to be transferred to the
place of subsequent FIR which is not in accordance with
the reasoning contained in the judgment. Therefore, it
is simply a clarification/modification of the order so
that the subsequent investigation may be clubbed with
the previous FIR and the investigation in connection
thereto in terms of the recitals in the judgment.
3

--- Page 286 ---
9. We  are  informed  that  there  is  no  stay  on
investigation in FIR No. 30/2019, PS Economic Offences
Wing (EOW), Delhi.
10. Accordingly, it is directed that FIR No. 439/2024,
PS  Sector  65  Gurugram,  Haryana  be  clubbed  and
transferred to FIR No. 30/2019, PS Economic Offences
Wing (EOW), Delhi.
11. The judgment and order dated 18.05.2026 passed in
Writ Petition (Crl.) No.67 of 2025 stands modified
accordingly.
12. The Miscellaneous Application stands disposed of.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
           
       
       .......................J.
   (PANKAJ MITHAL)          
......................J.
  (PRASANNA B. VARALE)
 NEW DELHI 
 21st May, 2026
    GA 
4

--- Page 287 ---

as also the deliberations of the Committee. The same is taken on
record.

designate Ms. Rita Jha, learned Advocate-on-Record (2995) to assist

the pipeline and the Committee may soon be able to come up with an
initial draft of its proposals for which, prayer is made that the
matter be listed after the ensuing summer vacation of the Court.
5. Accordingly, the matter be listed on 04.08.2026 at 02:30 p.m.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
(CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026)
(@SLP (CRL.) NO. 8559/2026)
SACHIN JAIN                         Appellant(s)
                      VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH               Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Heard.
2. Leave granted.
3. Appellant who is arraigned as an accused No. 6
in FIR No. 30/2026 registered at Police Station
Ganj, District Raipur for the offence punishable
under Sections 112(2) of Bharatiya Nyay Sanhita,
2023 and Section 7 of the Chhattisgarh Gambling
(Prohibition) Act, 2022, is seeking for being
enlarged on bail.
4. Prosecution allege that during a raid conducted
on 31.01.2026, six accused persons were found
1

--- Page 301 ---
operating online betting through two different
websites resulting in recovery of an amount of Rs.
37,50,000/-  (Thirty  Seven  Lakhs  and  Fifty
Thousands Only) lakhs, multiple mobile phones and
vehicles and an amount of Rs. 6,00,000/- (six
lakhs)  and  one  mobile  phone  were  allegedly
recovered from the appellant.
5. The trial Court and the High Court rejected the
bail primarily on the ground of seriousness of
organized online gambling, recovery of cash and
mobile  phones  alleged  incriminating  telephone
chats and the appellant’s prior gambling related
criminal antecedents. Hence, appellant is before
this Court.
6. Perusal of the records would indicate that co-
accused  Piyush  Jain  and  Kamal  Raghwani  were
granted regular bail and we do not find that
appellant is on parity with the said accused.
However, the material collected during the course
of the investigation attributable to the appellant
will have to be established by the prosecution
2

--- Page 302 ---
during the course of the trial. Chargesheet has
been  filed  on  completion  of  investigation.
Appellant would not be required for any further
investigation. Having regard to his period of
custody from 31.01.2026. We are of the considered
view, that the appellant is entitled for being
enlarged on bail.
7. Hence, we allow this appeal, set aside the
impugned order and direct the appellant to be
released  on  regular  bail  on  such  terms  and
conditions as the jurisdictional trial Court may
impose.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of. 
..................J
[ARAVIND KUMAR]
....................J
[PRASANNA B. VARALE]
New Delhi;
26 May, 2026.

1. Heard. 
2. Leave granted.
3. The appellants have been arraigned as
accused in FIR No.89/2025 registered for the
offences  punishable  under  Sections  191(2),
191(3), 190, 126(2), 115(2), 262, 263, 121(1),
132, 109, 158 and 351(2) of the Bharatiya
Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 for seeking for being
enlarged on bail. 
4. The gist of the prosecution case is that
PSI Santosh Kumar Rajak proceeded to arrest
three accused persons in FIR No.142 dated
28.05.2024 and at that point of time the
1

--- Page 306 ---
appellants and their family members are said
to have attacked the police force, injuring
some of the police personnel and also aided
the accused persons to escape.
5. Since the learned counsel appearing for
the respondent – State submitted that the
appellants were not cooperating. by interim
order  dated  16.04.2026,  we  directed  the
appellants to cooperate with the investigation
and subject to the same, interim protection
was granted from any coercive steps being
taken against the appellants. 
6. Learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent – State submits that pursuant to
the same, appellants have appeared before the
Investigating Officer. 
7. In that view of the matter, we are of
considered  view  that  interim  protection
granted deserves to be made absolute subject
to the condition that appellants shall mark
their  attendance  before  the  jurisdictional
2

--- Page 307 ---
police  thrice  a  week,  i.e.,  every  Monday
between  10:00  a.m.  to  12:00  p.m.,  every
Thursday between 11:00 a.m. to 02:00 p.m. and
every Saturday between 03:00 p.m. to 05:00
p.m.  without fail and also on the condition
that appellants shall appear before the trial
court on all dates of hearing except when
exempted  for  any  specific  reason.  The
Investigating  Officer  shall  release  the
appellants on these conditions and subject to
such other and further conditions as he deems
fit to impose. Accordingly, the appeal stands
disposed of. 
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall
stand disposed of.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

KAPILKUMAR KANTILAL JAIN                 APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
          STATE OF MAHARASHTRA                     RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
28.01.2026 in ABA No.3596 of 2024, titled “ Kapilkumar
Kantilal Jain Vs. State Of Maharashtra ”, passed by the
High Court of Judicature at Bombay.
3. On  28.04.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the  judgment  and  order
dated  28.01.2026  in  ABA  No.  3596/2024  passed  by  the
High Court of Judicature at Bombay  
2. We have heard learned counsel for the petitioner.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner submits that prior to
the  registration  of  the  FIR,  the  petitioner  had  already
submitted  a  complaint  to  the  concerned  Police  Station,
which is evident from Annexures P-3 and P-4 annexed to
the present petition.
1

--- Page 316 ---
4. Issue notice, returnable on 26.05.2026.
5.  Mr.  Aaditya  Aniruddha  Pande,  learned  Advocateon-
Record accepts notice on behalf of the respondent/State of
Maharashtra. Hence, the formal service of notice on the
respondent-State of Maharashtra is waived.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner to serve a copy of the
present  petition  to  learned  counsel  for  the
respondent/State of Maharashtra forthwith.
7.  Let  counter  affidavit  be  filed  within  three  weeks.
Rejoinder affidavit thereto, if any, be filed before the next
date of listing.
8.  In  the  event  of  arrest  in  connection  with  F.I.R.  No.
73/2024,  dated  24.09.2024,  Police  Station Cyber,  Nashik
City, for  the  offences punishable  under  Sections 318(4),
319(2) and 61(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and
Section 66D of the Information Technology Act, 2000, the
petitioner  shall  be  released  on  bail  on  the  appropriate
terms and conditions as may be fixed by the Investigating
Officer/Arresting Officer. However, in the attending facts,
one  of  the  conditions  shall  necessarily  be  that  if  the
petitioner possesses a passport, the same shall be deposited
before the competent authority/Court till the conclusion of
the Trial or directed otherwise.
9. The petitioner shall make himself available before the
Investigating Officer on 11.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and on all
such dates as he may be required.
2

--- Page 317 ---
10. Needless to add, till the investigation is not complete in
all respects, the petitioner would fully cooperate and if the
challan/charge  sheet  is  filed,  he  would  maintain  good
conduct and not attempt to influence any of the witnesses
in any manner till the completion of the trial”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 28.04.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
3

--- Page 318 ---
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 28.01.2026 is
set aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.

1. Leave granted.

passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna.

“1. The  petitioner  challenges  the
judgment and order dated 26.02.2026 in
Criminal  Miscellaneous  No.89165/2025
passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Patna. 
2. We have heard learned counsel for
the petitioner.
3.  Issue  notice,  returnable  on
26.05.2026.
4.  Dasti  service,  in  addition,  is
permitted. Let steps for service be taken
1

--- Page 321 ---
within one (01) week.
5. Mr. Samir Ali Khan, learned counsel,
accepts notice on behalf of respondent
no.1/State. Hence, the formal service of
notice on the said respondent is waived.
6. Learned counsel for the petitioner
to serve a copy of the present petition
to learned counsel for respondent no.1
within a period of one (01) week.
7. Respondent No.1 shall file counter
affidavit  within  three  (03)  weeks.
Rejoinder affidavit thereto, if any, be
filed before the next date of listing.
8. We make it clear that no further
opportunity  will  be  granted  for  this
purpose.
9. In the notice itself, let it be
mentioned  that  respondent  no.2  is
required to file the counter affidavit
and  reply  to  the  interlocutory
application(s), if any, before the next
date of listing.
10. In the event of arrest in connection
with  Crime  No.5793026250037/2025  of
Dhansoi Police Station, Buxar District,
Bihar, the petitioner shall be released
on  bail  on  the  appropriate  terms  and
conditions  as  may  be  fixed  by  the
Investigating  Officer/Arresting Officer.
However, in the attending facts, one of
the conditions shall necessarily be that
if the petitioner possesses a passport,
the same shall be deposited before the
Competent  Authority/Court,  till  the
conclusion  of  the  Trial  or  directed
2

--- Page 322 ---
otherwise.

--- Page 335 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.      /2026
@ SLP (CRL) NO.16118/2025
ANIRUDDH SINGH CHAUHAN ..... APPELLANTS
              VERSUS
STATE OF MADHYA PRADESH & ANR. ..... RESPONDENT
O R D E R
1. Heard. 
2. Leave granted. 
3. The appellant sought for quashing of the proceedings pending
against him by virtue of FIR No.85/2017 registered for the
offences punishable under Sections 409 and 420 of the Indian
Penal Code, 1860 and subsequent compromise having been entered
into between appellant and the complainant. 
4. The facts in brief are as under:
The complainant – respondent No.2 held a current account in
Canara Bank, Gwalior Branch in the name of Sai Medicals and his
wife  also  had  a  Saving  Bank  Account  in  the  same  bank.
Complainant had applied for a loan in his wife’s name. After
being informed by the Bank Manager that he could not get the
loan  due  to  existing  overdraft  facility  availed  by  the
1

--- Page 336 ---
appellant, on 30.05.2016, the complainant discovered that the
loan was sanctioned in the name of his wife and on the same day,
Rs.6,00,000/- was transferred from his wife’s account to his
firm account and then transferred to M/s. Om Sai Property
contending that neither he nor his wife had authorized these
transactions and the relevant documents lacked their signatures,
a complaint came to be lodged resulting in FIR 85/2017 referred
to hereinsupra being registered against the Bank Manager Mr.
Sachin Soni. 
The complainant sought to arraign the appellant as an
additional accused by filing an application under Section 319 of
the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (“CrPC”) which came to be
rejected  by  the  trial  court  by  order  dated  15.02.2023.
Complainant  filed  one  more  application  which  resulted  in
appellant being summoned and was arraigned as an additional
accused by order dated 23.07.2024 for allegedly transferring
Rs.6,00,000/- to M/s. Om Sai Properties traceable to appellant.
Subsequently, the appellant and the complainant have settled the
dispute and full amount of Rs.6,00,000/- was repaid to the
complainant via Demand Draft and as such this was also brought
to the notice of the High Court while assailing the initiation
of proceedings against the appellant, the compromise entered
into between the accused and the complainant was pressed for as
the basis for quashing of the proceedings. The High Court by the
2

--- Page 337 ---
impugned order rejected the said contention by opining that
manipulation of the bank records is a serious offence and it is
against the society and, therefore, the prosecution was not
quashed. 
5. In order to ascertain the factual position, this Court had
ordered notice on the complainant and as per the Office Report,
the same was duly served on the second respondent – complainant.
However,  this  Court  not  being  satisfied  with  such  report
insisted for the State to serve the complainant through the
jurisdictional SHO. Pursuant to same, an affidavit has been
filed enclosing the service report which would clearly indicate
that notice of this petition has been served on the complainant
who is arraigned as respondent No.2 and yet he has not appeared
or represented through counsel. In that view of the matter, we
do not propose to keep this matter pending and having heard the
learned counsels appearing for the parties, we notice that an
offence under Section 420 of the IPC is a compoundable offence
and the ingredients of Section 409 of the IPC is lacking in the
instant case as is evident from the application for summoning
the appellant as an accused. 
6. Having regard to the fact that the ignition for a criminal
prosecution  being  launched  against  the  appellant  was  the
application filed under Section 319 of the CrPC and in the
instant case, namely, on the basis that the amount transferred
3

--- Page 338 ---
from the complainant’s wife’s account was traceable to the
account of the appellant, he was arraigned as an accused. The
said amount having been refunded and compromise having been
entered into between them and there being no possibility of
order of conviction being imposed on the appellant, we are of
the considered view that continuation of the present criminal
prosecution would only be an exercise in futility and would be
an abuse of the process of the Court. As such, we allow this
appeal and the proceedings initiated against the appellant on
the strength of FIR No.85/2017 qua the appellant stands quashed.
However, we make it clear that right of the Bank if any to
recover, the amount is kept intact and Bank would be at liberty
to proceed to recover the said amount in accordance with law. 
7. Pending application(s), if any, stands consigned to records.
     ..................J.
(ARAVIND KUMAR)
..................J.
(PRASANNA B. VARALE)
NEW DELHI;
MAY 25, 2026.
4

--- Page 339 ---

--- Page 345 ---
1
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.       /2026
  [@ SLP [CRL.] NO.6203/2026]
DEEPAK KERKETTA                  Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF CHHATTISGARH              Respondent(s)
O R D E R
Leave granted.
The appellant stands convicted by the Trial
Court  in  Sessions  Trial  No.24/2023  for  the
offences punishable under Sections 323, 341, 307,
506 Part II of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 (for
short ‘IPC’) and Sections 25 and 27 of the Arms
Act,  1959.  He  has  been  sentenced  to  undergo
rigorous imprisonment for a period of 10 years
along with payment of fine. The High Court was
pleased to reject the application for suspension
of sentence preferred by the appellant.  Aggrieved
by the same, the appellant is before us.

--- Page 346 ---
2
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
submitted that the evidence available on record
would  clearly  enure  to  the  benefit  of  the
appellant. Out of the total sentence of 10 years
awarded  to  the  appellant  for  the  offence
punishable under Section 307 of the IPC, he has
already undergone incarceration for a period of
one and a half years.  
Though, a strong objection has been raised
by  the  learned  counsel  appearing  for  the
respondent-State,  the  fact  remains  that  the
appellant has already undergone incarceration for
one and a half years. The appeal of the appellant
before the High Court is of the year 2025. We
also take note of the judgment rendered by the
Trial Court along with the allegations made.
Suffice it is to state that the appellant is
entitled for suspension of sentence, during the
pendency of his appeal before the High Court.

--- Page 347 ---
3
Accordingly, the sentence of the appellant
is suspended and he is granted bail, during the
pendency of his appeal before the High Court, on
the  terms  and  conditions  subject  to  the
satisfaction of the concerned Trial Court.
The appeal stands allowed, accordingly.
Pending application(s), if any, shall stand
disposed of.
...................J.
[M.M. SUNDRESH]
...................J.
[NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH

Leave granted.
The  appellant  apprehends  his  arrest  in
connection with FIR No.165/2025 dated 27.05.2025,
registered  at  Police  Station  Vishwavidyalaya,
District – Rewa, Madhya Pradesh, for the offences
punishable under Sections 376(2)(n) and 323 of the
Indian Penal Code, 1860. 
We have heard the learned counsel appearing
for the parties.
Learned counsel appearing for the appellant
submitted that this is a case wherein the parties
met each other through Facebook. The prosecutrix
is not only a divorced woman, but also much older
1

--- Page 356 ---
than the appellant. It is only when it came to be
known that the appellant is likely to get married
that a complaint was filed by her. Considering the
nature  of  the  allegations  involved,  custodial
interrogation of the appellant is not required. 
On the facts of the case, we find that
custodial interrogation is certainly not required,
especially  when  the  appellant  has  joined  the
investigation  pursuant  to  our  order  dated
03.09.2025.
Considering the above, we are inclined to
grant  anticipatory  bail  to  the  appellant.  
Accordingly, the impugned order is set aside,
and the appellant is granted anticipatory bail on
terms and conditions to the satisfaction of the
concerned Trial Court.
2

--- Page 357 ---

    IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CIVIL APPEAL NO.        OF 2026 
(Arising out of SLP (C) No.36239 of 2025)
  MEHUL BALMUKUNDBHAI TRIVEDI                       APPELLANT(s)
                               VERSUS
  DEPUTY EXECUTIVE ENGINEER & ANR.                 RESPONDENT(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
15.07.2025 in R/Letters Patent Appeal No. 114 of 2025
passed by the High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad.
3. The appellant, a workman engaged as a driver with the
respondent  authorities  since  02.07.2009,  was  orally
terminated from service on 18.06.2011 without compliance of
the mandatory provisions of Section 25F of the Industrial
Disputes Act, 1947. The Labour Court, Amreli, by award
dated 29.05.2014, held the termination to be illegal but
awarded  compensation  of  Rs.  50,000/-  instead  of
1

--- Page 361 ---
reinstatement.
4. The learned Single Judge of the High Court of Gujarat, by
judgment dated 20.06.2024, directed reinstatement of the
appellant with continuity of service but without back
wages. The Division Bench, however, in Letters Patent
Appeal  No.  114  of  2025,  set  aside  the  order  of
reinstatement  and  enhanced  the  compensation  to  Rs.
2,00,000/-.
5. Having heard learned counsel for the parties and upon
perusal of the record, we are of the considered view that
the interest of justice would be best served by further
enhancing  the  compensation.  The  appellant  has  been
litigating since 2011, and the record discloses vacancies
in the post of driver even subsequent to his termination.
In these circumstances, mere compensation of Rs. 2,00,000/-
would not be adequate.
6. Accordingly, the compensation payable to the appellant is
enhanced to Rs. 5,00,000/- (Rupees Five Lakhs only).
7. It is noted that a sum of Rs. 50,000/- already stands
deposited by the respondents before the High Court. The
said amount, together with accrued interest, shall be
released forthwith in favour of the appellant(s).
8. The balance amount of Rs. 4,50,000/- (Rupees Four Lakhs
2

--- Page 362 ---
Fifty Thousand only) shall be remitted directly into the
bank account of the appellant(s) by the respondents within
a period of four weeks from the date of uploading of this
order. The appellant(s) shall furnish the requisite bank
account particulars to learned counsel for the respondents
without delay.
9. In the event of default, the appellant(s) shall be entitled
to interest on the outstanding amount at the rate awarded
by the Labour Court/Tribunal.
10. The appeal stands allowed in the above terms. Pending
applications, if any, shall stand disposed of.
          
                                     ..........................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
                    .............................J.
(NONGMEIKAPAM KOTISWAR SINGH)
NEW DELHI
APRIL 09, 2026
3

1. Earlier,  this  Court  vide order  dated
02.04.2025 had allowed the Civil Appeal No. 4667
of 2025 and after setting aside the impugned
order therein, decreeing the suit for specific
performance,  it  was  provided  that  the
defendant/appellant  (petitioner  herein)  would
pay a total amount of Rs. 2,50,00,000/- (Rupees
Two Crores and Fifty Lakhs Only).
2. As the defendant/appellant failed to pay the
said amount, the plaintiff (respondent herein),
initiated  execution  proceedings.  In  the
execution proceedings, after giving certain time
to the defendant/petitioner, the executing Court
SLP (C) No. 16582 of 2026    1

--- Page 366 ---
vide order  dated  20.12.2025  attached  the
property in question and by the impugned order
dated 17.03.2026, the said order was affirmed.
Aggrieved by the same, the present petition has
been filed.
3. Learned counsel for the petitioner(s) submits
that the petitioner(s) are not in a position to
pay the amount which they had agreed while
appeal was being decided by the order dated
02.04.2025.
4. In that view of the matter, the only option
left to this Court is to withdraw and recall the
order dated 02.04.2025 and dismiss the appeal
preferred by the defendants i.e. Appeal No. 4667
of 2025.
5. Accordingly, the Special Leave Petition is
disposed of.

Appeal No. 4667 of 2025 is recalled. The order
and judgments of the Court below decreeing the
suit for specific performance stand revived. The
Civil Appeal is dismissed.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
Criminal Appeal  No(s).         /2026
SLP (Crl.) No(s). 7876/2026
RAMCHARAN                                          Appellant(s)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF UTTAR PRADESH                             Respondent(s)
O R D E R 
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3.This appeal arises from an order dated
12.11.2025  passed  by  the  High  Court  of
Judicature  at  Allahabad  rejecting  the
regular bail prayer of the appellant in
connection  with  Crime  No.  137/2020,
registered at P.S. Radhauli, District Basti.
4. The submission of the learned counsel for
the appellant is that the appellant was not
named  in  the  First  Information  Report
(“FIR”); and he was arraigned as an accused
by  taking  recourse  to  the  powers  under
1

--- Page 381 ---
Section  319  of  the  Code  of  Criminal
Procedure, 1973 (“CrPC”). It has also been
submitted that there is no specific role
attributed  to  the  appellant  and  the
appellant by now has suffered incarceration
of about 3 years, pending trial. It has also
been submitted that he is aged 78 years and
there is no possibility of him misusing the
bail.
5. The  learned  Counsel  for  the  State-
respondent has opposed the prayer for bail
but could not dispute that the appellant was
arraigned  as  an  accused  in  exercise  of
powers under Section 319 CrPC and that he
was not named in the FIR. Besides, it is not
disputed  that  the  appellant  is  an  aged
person and has suffered incarceration of
nearly 3 years pending trial. 
6.  Having  regard  to  the  above,  without
expressing any opinion on the merits of the
case, we are of the view that the appellant
is entitled to be released on bail at this
stage.
2

--- Page 382 ---
7. Accordingly, the appeal is allowed. The
order passed by the High Court rejecting the
regular bail prayer of the appellant is set
aside.
8.The appellant shall be released on bail on
such terms and conditions as the trial court
may deem fit to impose in the facts and
circumstances of the case.
9.Pending  application(s),  if  any,  shall
stand disposed of.
  
………………………………………………………………..J
[MANOJ MISRA]
………………………………………………………………..J
[MANMOHAN]
New Delhi;
May 26, 2026.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
IA No. 160215/2026
1. This application has been moved by the State of Manipur
briefly narrating the unprecedented ethnic and civil unrest that
has taken place in the State.  Mr. Rajiv Singh, IPS of Tripura
Cadre was appointed as Director General of Police in Manipur on
02.06.2023. His term is coming to an end on 31.05.2026.  In the
peculiar facts and circumstances prevailing in the State, the
prayer has been made to appoint the Director General of Police from
outside Manipur cadre.
2. We find merit in the prayer.  The application is allowed.  The
State of Manipur is permitted to appoint the Deputy Inspector
General of Police from outside Manipur Cadre.  
(NITIN TALREJA)                              (PREETHI DILEEP KUMAR)
ASTT. REGISTRAR-cum-PS                        ASSISTANT REGISTRAR
5

--- Page 390 ---
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO. 2528/2026
(arising out of SLP(Crl.) No. 13607/2025
(@ Diary No. 45782/2025)
ANIL BANSAL ..... APPELLANT(S)
VERSUS
THE STATE (NCT OF DELHI) & ORS. ..... RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant is aggrieved by the order dated 25.10.2024
passed by the High Court of Delhi in Writ Petition (Criminal)
No. 2954/2024.
3. The said writ petition was filed by respondent No.4 herein,
assailing the three notices dated 12.09.2024 issued by the
Investigating Officer under Sections 91, 160 and  102 of the
Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973. By the impugned order, the
High Court disposed of the writ petition on the very first
date, directing respondent No.4, the petitioner in the writ
petition, to furnish to the Investigating Officer a bond in
the sum of 2.13 crore (Rupees Two Crore Thirteen Lakhs only)₹
within a time frame and subject to the same, the freezing of
the bank accounts by the Investigating Officer, by way of the
1

--- Page 391 ---
notices dated 12.09.2024, was cancelled. The  Investigating
Officer was directed to withdraw the same under written
intimation to the banks in question.
4. Having heard the learned counsel for the appearing parties, we
find that the notices dated 12.09.2024, impugned before the
High Court by respondent No.4, specifically mentioned the fact
that steps were being taken by the  Investigating Officer in
relation to First Information Report (FIR) No.66 of 2024 dated
07.02.2024,  which  was  registered  at  the  behest  of  the
appellant, Anil Bansal, against the accused persons. 
5. Having impugned the aforestated notices, it was incumbent upon
respondent No.4 to make Anil Bansal, the complainant in the
FIR, a party to the writ petition. It, however, failed to do
so. The High Court also remained impervious to this crucial
lapse on the part of respondent No.4, whereby an order was
passed adverse to the interest of the appellant behind his
back.
6. The impugned order is, therefore, liable to be set aside on
this short ground, as the very filing of the writ petition in
the manner it was filed, amounted to an abuse of process by
respondent No.4.
7. The appeal is allowed, setting aside the impugned order dated
25.10.2024.  In consequence the freezing of the bank accounts,
under the impugned notices dated 12.09.2024, shall revive as
on date and whatever amounts are lying in the said accounts
shall not be allowed to be accessed by the account holders
2

--- Page 392 ---
till consideration of the application filed by the appellant
in Misc. Criminal/40794/2024, titled “ State vs Padmesh &
Ors.”, on the file of the learned Chief Judicial Magistrate,
Saket, New Delhi.
8. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed of.
........................J.
    (SANJAY KUMAR)
........................J.
(K. VINOD CHANDRAN)
NEW DELHI;
May 14, 2026.
3

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO(s).         OF 2026
(arising out of SLP (Crl.) No(s).7411/2026)
DEEPAK KUMAR @ DEEPAK YADAV                        APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
STATE OF BIHAR                                     RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellant challenges the judgment and order dated
12.03.2026 in Criminal Miscellaneous No.66141/2025 passed
by the High Court of Judicature at Patna.
3. On  27.04.2026,  this  Court  passed  the  following
order:-
“1.  The  petitioner  challenges  the
judgment and order dated 12.03.2026 in
Criminal  Miscellaneous  No.66141/2025
passed by the High Court of Judicature at
Patna. 
2. We have heard learned counsel for
the petitioner.
3. Issue  notice,  returnable  on
25.05.2026.
4. Mr. Manish Kumar, learned counsel,
accepts  notice  on  behalf  of  the
respondent/State.  Hence,  the  formal
service of notice on the respondent is
waived.
5. Learned counsel for the petitioner
to serve a copy of the present petition
to  learned  counsel  for  the  respondent
forthwith.
6. Let  counter  affidavit  be  filed
1

--- Page 400 ---
within  three  (03)  weeks.  Rejoinder
affidavit  thereto,  if  any,  be  filed
before the next date of listing.
7. We make it clear that no further
opportunity  will  be  granted  for  this
purpose.
8. In the event of arrest in connection
with Crime No.5790020250137 of 2025 of
Kako Police Station, Jehanabad District,
Bihar, the petitioner shall be released
on  bail  on  the  appropriate  terms  and
conditions  as  may  be  fixed  by  the
Investigating  Officer/Arresting Officer.
However, in the attending facts, one of
the conditions shall necessarily be that
if the petitioner possesses a passport,
the same shall be deposited before the
Competent  Authority/Court,  till  the
conclusion  of  the  Trial  or  directed
otherwise.
9. The petitioner shall make himself
available  before  the  Investigating
Officer on 04.05.2026 at 10:00 a.m. and
on all such dates as he may be required.
10. Needless  to  add,  till  the
investigation  is  not  complete  in  all
respects,  the  petitioner  would  fully
cooperate,  and  if  the  challan/charge
sheet is filed, he would maintain good
conduct and not attempt to influence any
of the witnesses in any manner till the
completion of the trial.”
4. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and
also perused the materials available on record.
5. It is not in dispute that the conditions stand fully
complied with; the appellant has cooperated during the
investigation; he has not threatened or intimidated any
of  the  witnesses;  or  has  tried  to  influence  the
investigation in any manner.
6. We have noticed the nature of crime and the manner in
2

--- Page 401 ---
which it was allegedly committed.
7. We allow the present appeal by confirming the order
dated 27.04.2026, passed by this Court, with a direction
to the appellant to continue to cooperate during the
investigation/trial  and  not  to  take  any  unnecessary
adjournment. Also, the appellant shall report before the
Investigating Officer every alternate Monday, between 10
a.m. and 11 a.m., till such time the investigation is
complete in all respects.
8. If the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial
Court feels that the investigation/trial is delayed on
account of the appellant’s conduct, it shall be open to
the Investigating Officer/Arresting Officer/Trial Court
to take appropriate steps in accordance with the law,
including cancellation of bail.
9. The impugned judgment and order dated 12.03.2026 is
set aside.
10. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
..................J.
(SANJAY KAROL)
..................J.
(AUGUSTINE GEORGE MASIH)

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA

MUNIYAMMA & ANR.                                   APPELLANT(S)
                                VERSUS
MUNIRATHNAMMA & ORS.                               RESPONDENT(S)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. The appellants challenge the judgment and order dated
13.06.2025 in RSA No.1977/2021 (PAR/INJ) passed by the
High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru.
3. As per the Office Report dated 22.05.2026, mediation
inter se the parties has been successful. We have perused
the Mediation Report dated 04.05.2026 along with the
Settlement Agreement annexed to the main paper book.
4. Learned counsel for the parties submitted that all
the disputes and differences between the parties have
been amicably resolved, and the parties have decided to
part ways on certain terms and conditions. Accordingly,
appropriate  and  necessary  orders  may  be  passed  in
accordance therewith.
5. We  appreciate  the  efforts  put  in  by  Mr.  B.C.
Thiruvengadam, learned senior counsel, who has mediated
between the parties, and also learned counsel for the
parties in helping them arrive at an amicable settlement.
1

--- Page 404 ---
6. The terms of the Settlement Agreement reads as under:
“……  1.  The  respondents  have  agreed
unconditionally  to  give  one  acre  of
agricultural land out of the Survey No.24
situated at Hithalahalli village, Kasaba
Hobli,  Sidlagatta  Taluk,  out  of  four
acres  as  described  in  item  1  of  the
plaint as well as the appeal memo and
further have agreed to give a sum of
Rupees  Twenty  Lakhs  only  to  the
Petitioners as full and final settlement
and  claim  with  respect  to  the  suit
schedule properties.
2. The Respondents shall ensure that the
Petitioners have reasonable access to the
said one acre of land, from the road
without let or hinderance to both the
parties.
3. The Respondents shall pay the sum of
Rupees Twenty lakhs within a period of
six months from the date of order of the
Hon'ble  Supreme  Court,  without  seeking
further extension of time.
4.  The  Petitioners  give  up  all  other
rights and claim with respect to the suit
schedule property and the same is final
and binding on the petitioners and their
legal heirs.
5. The 1 st Petitioner is represented by
her daughter, the 2nd petitioner who is
her power of attorney holder, copy of
which  is  enclosed  herewith  along  with
attested copy of their aadhar cars and
duly attested by their counsels. …..”
7. In view of the above, the following directions are
issued:
i) All the legal proceedings pending  inter se  the
parties, if any, shall stand closed;
ii) Decree be drawn up accordingly;
iii) It goes without saying that both the parties
will continue to abide by the terms of the Settlement
2

--- Page 405 ---
Agreement without any exception;
iv) The impugned judgment/order dated 13.06.2025 is
modified accordingly;
v) The  terms  of  the  Settlement  Agreement  are
binding upon the parties. The parties undertake to
abide by the same; and
vi) The  parties,  through  their  learned  counsel
present in the Court, have been made aware of the
consequences of breach of such terms, including the
initiation of contempt proceedings.
8. Accordingly, the present appeal stands disposed of.
9. Pending application(s), if any, shall also stand
disposed of.

          UPON hearing the counsel the Court made the following
                             O R D E R
1) The present petition has been filed for the alleged
non-compliance  of  the  directions  of  the  High  Court,
confirmed by this Court vide order dated 22.08.2024 in Civil
Appeal No. 9940 of 2016 within two months.  
1

--- Page 408 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) No. 193/2026 in C.A. No. 9940/2016
2) Vide order dated 08.04.2026, n otice was issued asking
compliance.  On  29.04.2026,  it  was  directed  that  the
compliance affidavit be filed within two weeks otherwise,
the Director General, ITBP, shall personally remain present.
3) Compliance affidavit has been filed on 07.05.2026.
Upon perusal, it reveals that pursuant to the order of the
High Court and of this Court, joining was permitted within
the time as specified but the other compliances have not
been duly made.  
4) The last order passed by the Office of the Commandant,
14th Battalion,  dated  17.12.2025,  indicates  certain
compliances after one year and three months; para 7 thereof
is reproduced as under:
“7. Therefore, in compliance to the judgments
passed by the Hon’ble High Court of Delhi and
the Hon’ble Supreme Court on 17.08.2015 and
22.08.2024 respectively, and as per Order No.
5163-5255 dated 23.10.2024 of 14 th Battalion,
ITBP  Force,  the  punishment  of  dismissal  of
Regt.  No.  900231262,  Head  Constable  (GD)
Sanjeev Chaudhary is modified to “reduction to
the induction level of the pay scale/initial
stage of pay enjoyed by him as Head Constable”,
and the following orders are hereby passed
(a) The period of service rendered in the rank
of Head Constable shall be counted for all
other consequential benefits.
(b)  The  period  of  absence  from  leave  from
28.12.2012 to 01.04.2013 totaling 95 days is
regularized as follows: 
i.  The  period  from  28.12.2012  to
01.03.2013  (64  days)  is  regularized
against the available 64 days Earned Leave
in the individual’s leave account. 
ii.  The  period  from  02.03.2013  to
01.04.2013 (31 days) is regularized as
Extraordinary  Leave  (EOL)  without
2

--- Page 409 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) No. 193/2026 in C.A. No. 9940/2016
cumulative effect. 
(c) The intervening period from the date of
dismissal from service to the date of rejoining
duty,  i.e.,  from  02.04.2013  to  15.10.2024
totaling 11 years, 06 months and 15 days, is
regulated as “NO WORK, NO PAY” by exercising
the powers conferred under Rules 54 and 54-A of
FRSR Part-I.”
5) Thereafter, by another order of the same date the
following observations have been made: 
“2. The pay of the individual shall be refixed on
the orders of regularization of 14 th Bn for period
from 01.04.2013 to 15.10.2024.
3. The said personnel shall be entitled to Dearness
Allowance and other allowances as admissible under
the rules issued by the Central Government from
time to time. 
4. The Pay Fixation of above personnel will be
subjected to post audit by the Internal Audit or
the PAO in terms of the instructions issued vide
Govt. of India (DOP&T) O.M. 18/03/2015 (Pay-1)
dated 03.10.2022. 
5.  This  has  the  approval  of  Deputy  Inspector
General, CRO on Para No. 06 of Noting No. 04 of
Single  File  No.  16013/2/2025/HC  (GD)  Sanjeev
Chaudhary.”
 
6) As such payment of salary was made on 12.01.2026.  
7) After perusal of the aforesaid orders, it is not clear
at the time of reinstatement what was the minimum pay akin
to other similar employees to which payment has been paid.
It is also not clear whether while making the payment of
Rs.9,71,241/- after one year and three months of joining,
whether the pay of the applicant has been duly fixed and
applicable DA has been paid thereon; calculation to that
effect is not available.  Simultaneously, the issue as
3

--- Page 410 ---
CONMT.PET.(C) No. 193/2026 in C.A. No. 9940/2016
raised before us that the other Head Constables are getting

not on record, therefore, the affidavit only indicates the
partial compliance.  
8) It is surprising in absence of full compliance placed

recourse by the officer cannot be appreciated.  
9) In any case, let rejoinder affidavit be filed, within
two weeks, and if it is found that the order of the High
Court and this Court has not been duly complied with,
appropriate orders taking the said conduct on record, may be
passed.  
10) This order be also communicated to the DGP concerned
for further recourse.

ITEM NO.29               COURT NO.13               SECTION XIV
               S U P R E M E  C O U R T  O F  I N D I A
                       RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS
Petition(s) for Special Leave to Appeal (C)  No(s).  3712/2025
[Arising out of impugned final judgment and order dated  11-12-2024
in CMM No. 3757/2024 passed by the High Court of Delhi at New 
Delhi]
NIKHIL KAPUR                                       Petitioner(s)
                                VERSUS
MANAT LAMBA                                        Respondent(s)
IA No. 82287/2025 - APPROPRIATE ORDERS/DIRECTIONS
IA No. 104532/2025 - APPROPRIATE ORDERS/DIRECTIONS
IA No. 33806/2025 - EXEMPTION FROM FILING C/C OF THE IMPUGNED 
JUDGMENT
IA No. 80971/2025 - MODIFICATION OF COURT ORDER
IA No. 82377/2025 - MODIFICATION OF COURT ORDER
IA No. 34063/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
IA No. 131638/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
IA No. 117048/2025 - PERMISSION TO FILE ADDITIONAL 
DOCUMENTS/FACTS/ANNEXURES
 
Date : 25-05-2026 This matter was called for hearing today.
CORAM :  HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE AHSANUDDIN AMANULLAH
         HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE R. MAHADEVAN
For Petitioner(s) :Mr. Vipin Sanghi, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Misha Rohatgi, AoR
                   Mr. Amulya Upadhyay, Adv.
                   Ms. Somyashree, Adv.
                   Mr. Shikhar Sarin, Adv.
                   
                   
For Respondent(s) :Mr. Rajiv Shakdher, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Geeta Luthra, Sr. Adv.
                   Ms. Shagufa Salim, AoR
                   Mr. Uday Pratap Singh, Adv.
                   Mr. Dennis Jacob, Adv.
                   Mr. Karan Khetani, Adv.
                   
                   
                             O R D E R
Heard learned Senior Counsel for the parties.

--- Page 412 ---
2. The petitioner is also present in court.
3. There  is  dispute  with  regard  to  whether  the  interim
arrangement  as  suggested/directed  by  the  learned  Mediator  of
visitation rights have been complied with by the respondent.
4. What has been submitted, the position is that the arrangement
could not be adhered to due to various factors for which each side
blames the other side.
5. We had also asked the learned Mediator for his views in the
matter. Learned counsel for the respondent has submitted the
interim report of the learned Mediator dated 25.05.2026. From the
same, it appears that though the marriage has irretrievably broken
and the parties are ready to work out the modalities of separation
and  they  are  also  not  yet  in  a  position  to  agree  on  the
modalities/arrangements of custody and visitation of their child.
The learned Mediator has indicated that the child requires Court-
supervised child counseling so as to make the child comfortable to
both the father and the mother.
6. Today after some discussions, it has been agreed between the
parties that the modalities as had been indicated by the learned
Mediator by way of interim measure relating to visitation rights be
put into actual action. The hitch which was coming in the same was
that according to the petitioner, the respondent used to accompany
the child for dropping her but after that, she used to keep sitting
in the house, which had an adverse impact on the child and very
soon, she would insist to return and thus, the petitioner was
unable to be with his daughter for the period which was specified
as per the said agreement.

--- Page 413 ---
7. Though the learned counsel for the respondent refutes the
position, but still to move forward, it has been agreed between the
parties that the respondent would drop the child at the residence
of the petitioner as per the schedule fixed by the learned mediator
and thereafter, would come to pick her up after the period
specified in the said order. Thereafter, the petitioner shall drop
her off at the residence of the respondent upon completion of the
period as specified in the order.
8. Let the same be strictly adhered to and followed. The matter
be listed on 14.07.2026 at 3.00 p.m. treating the matter as part-
heard.
9. As a suggestion has come from the learned Senior Counsel for
the petitioner that since the vacation has started and would
continue for the entire month of June, 2026 if the parties agree,
petitioner may be permitted to take his daughter on a short
vacation. We make no observation and leave it for the parties to
decide. We only indicate that if at all ultimately the parties have
to separate,  the Court would sincerely request them to make an
endeavour to lay the grounds for amicable settlement, especially
keeping in mind that in future there should be no acrimony between
them for ultimately the child cannot be cut from her blood
relations both with the petitioner and the respondent and for the
ultimate benefit of the child, which the Court presumes is at the
core of the intention of both the parties, we leave it for the
parties to agree to such decisions upon mutual consent. 
10. At this stage, it is desirable that as in the previous
proceedings, it was indicated that nearby residence would be

--- Page 414 ---
arranged for the respondent and the child to live in, which will be
provided by the petitioner and the respondent has given three
options, but no movement has been made on this front by the
petitioner  and  also  with  regard  to  return  of  the  personal
belongings  of  the  respondent,  which  are  still  lying  in  the
matrimonial home.
11. In the interest of justice, we request the parties to sort it
out and the parties should also remember that if they have
undertaken before this Court or among themselves for a certain
course of action, they are, if not bound by the order of the
Court(s),  but  morally  bound  to  comply  with  the  same.  Let
substantive progress be made in that front also before the next
date.
12. After the order was dictated, learned counsel have indicated
with regard to other issues, we only indicate that as far as return
of the personal belongings of the respondent is concerned as also
providing her with an alternative residence, parties are directed
to work out the same in the meantime.
13. It goes without saying that whatever interim arrangement the
parties work inter se would be the part and parcel of the ultimate
final settlement and consequently the part of the final order of
this Court.
(SACHIN KUMAR SRIVASTAVA)                       (ANJALI PANWAR)
   COURT MASTER (SH)                         ASSISTANT  REGISTRAR

Court, Saharanpur, Uttar Pradesh.

learned counsel for the respondent, and we have perused the  
record.

materials available on record, we are of the view that this  
Transfer Petition deserves to be allowed. Accordingly, Original  
Suit No.647 of 2025, pending before the learned Principal  
Judge, Family Court, Dehradun, Uttrakhand is transferred to  
learned  Principal  Judge,  Family  Court,  Saharanpur,  Uttar

of the case forthwith to the transferee Court.

IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA
CRIMINAL APPELLATE JURISDICTION
CRIMINAL APPEAL NO.        of 2026
[ARISING OUT OF SPECIAL LEAVE PETITION (CRL.)
NO.20856/2025]
ANSHU KUMAR                                  Appellant(s)
                           VERSUS
THE STATE OF BIHAR & ANR.                    Respondent(s)
O R D E R
1. Leave granted.
2. Heard learned counsel for the parties.
3. This appeal arises from an order dated 10.12.2025
passed by the High Court of Judicature at Patna rejecting
the anticipatory bail prayer of the appellant in connection
with Complaint Case No.331 of 2023.
4. On 19.12.2025, the following interim protection order
was passed:
“1. Issue notice, returnable in six weeks.
2. Mr. Anshul Narayan, learned counsel who
appears along with Mr. Prem Prakash, AOR
accepts notice on behalf of State of Bihar.
3. Issuance of notice upon the respondent –
State is dispensed with.
4.  In  the  meantime,  if  the  petitioner
1

--- Page 432 ---
submits bail bond to the satisfaction of the
court of Judicial Magistrate, First Class-
cum-Addl.  Munsif,  Sikarhana  Dhaka,  East
Champaran,  he  shall  not  be  taken  into
custody in connection with Complaint Case
No.331 of 2023.”
 
5. It is stated that pursuant to the interim order, the
appellant has already submitted bail bonds.
6. Having regard to the fact that the proceedings emanate
from a complaint, we are of the view that this appeal can
be disposed of by making the interim order absolute subject
to the condition that the appellant shall co-operate in the
proceedings.
7. Pending application(s), if any, shall stand disposed
of.
